animation.update_left: {{midVal}} < {{target}} (Too small), target is in the right half. Update Left = {{mid}} + 1
animation.update_right: {{midVal}} > {{target}} (Too large), target is in the left half. Update Right = {{mid}} - 1
animation.not_found: Search complete: Range is empty (Left > Right), target {{target}} not found

binary-search-beat-the-algorithm.title: Beat the Algorithm: Binary Search Number Guessing Duel

sections.content: The core of binary search is "each decision eliminates about half the possibilities." In a range of 1 to 100, theoretically at most ⌈log₂(100)⌉ ≈ 7 steps are needed to lock in the answer.

This interactive game lets you click on a number line to make your guess, simultaneously dueling against an AI shadow that "always picks the midpoint." Through the visual shrinking of the search interval and remaining probability hints, you can intuitively feel the convergence speed of O(log n).

algo-001.title: The Superpower Hidden Behind the Digital World — Binary Search

sections.heading: The Ultimate Magic of Simplification
sections.content: The core spirit of binary search is very simple: "Every step cuts out half the impossible options." It's like finding a specific word in a dictionary of 1 million pages — a linear search going page by page would take an enormous amount of time, but binary search can pinpoint the target in fewer than 20 steps. This exponential decay power of O(log n) makes it the top choice for handling massive amounts of data. From the Git Bisect tool engineers use to track down bugs, to the index queries at the bottom of databases, this intuitive strategy is the cornerstone of modern technology.

sections.heading: Unleashing GPU Potential: 3D Games and Parallel Computing
sections.content: When playing a visually stunning AAA game, the GPU must determine collisions between millions of rays and geometric models in milliseconds. According to a breakthrough by NVIDIA Research (2012), they used "Morton Codes" to compress complex 3D spatial coordinates into sortable one-dimensional segments. Then, the GPU's thousands of compute cores can work simultaneously, using "binary search" to quickly locate split points for their respective sections. This large-scale parallel processing technique allows the system to build massive spatial structures (such as BVH) all at once, completely unleashing the computational potential of high-end graphics cards and ensuring buttery-smooth rendering.

sections.heading: The Express Navigation of the Internet: IP Routing Lookup
sections.content: Binary search is also the "nervous system" of the global internet. Each network packet requires routers to determine the path from millions of rules in an extremely short time — this is called "Longest Prefix Matching (LPM)." A team at UCLA (2001) proposed a bold idea: rather than searching through a vast number of IP addresses, perform binary search on "prefix lengths." This breakthrough compressed the number of lookups to logarithmic levels (only 5 steps for IPv4), enabling global routers to forward data at hundreds of Gb/s, ensuring high-speed connectivity under any traffic pressure.

Scalable High-Speed Prefix Matching

relatedProblems.704: Classic algorithm: search for a target value in a sorted array (basic binary search)
relatedProblems.35: Variant: find the target or return where it would be inserted (lower bound concept)
relatedProblems.34: Advanced: find the first and last position of a target in a sorted array with duplicates

sections.heading: Core Concept
sections.content: Binary search is an **algorithm** for finding a target in a [[primary|sorted]] array. Like playing a guessing game, it repeatedly halves the **search range** to eliminate half the possibilities.
items: Requires a [[primary|sorted]] structure, since the ordering relationship determines whether to continue searching the left or right **boundary**
Calculates the **midpoint** and compares it to the target, then updates the **left boundary** or **right boundary** to eliminate half the [[primary|search space]]
Returns the **index** on a hit; if the **left boundary** exceeds the **right boundary**, the range is empty and the target [[primary|does not exist]]

sections.heading: Operation Complexity
sections.content: Binary search eliminates candidates by halving the interval each step, making it highly efficient. However, complexity varies between implementations and array insertion operations.
items: **Best-case search**: Target lands exactly at the midpoint for an instant hit, time complexity [[success|O(1)]]
**General search**: Halves the search space each round until exhausted, time complexity [[success|O(log n)]]
**Element insertion**: Locating the insertion point is fast, but shifting data in an array costs [[warning|O(n)]]
**Space usage**: Iterative approach is [[success|O(1)]]; recursive approach uses [[warning|O(log n)]] call stack space

sections.heading: What Can Be Optimized
sections.content: Binary search excels at quickly locating targets in **sorted** arrays, but is poorly suited for frequently changing data. When heavy insertion and deletion are needed, consider switching to an alternative structure.
items: **Static lookups**: Best for simple, infrequently changing **sorted** arrays where it delivers peak search performance
**Hidden monotonicity**: Not limited to numeric values — any problem with **monotonicity** (once a condition holds, it keeps holding) can apply this technique
**Frequent modifications**: Inserting into an array requires shifting elements to preserve order, making it unsuitable for high-churn scenarios
**Alternative structures**: When only exact-value access is needed with no range queries, a **dictionary** offers better lookup performance

sections.heading: Suitable Problem Types
sections.content: Any problem with monotonicity — e.g., if a capacity works, a larger one must too — can use binary search to eliminate half the candidates and converge quickly to the optimal answer.
items: **Finding boundary positions**: Locate the first or last occurrence of a target in a sorted array with duplicates
**Minimizing the maximum**: e.g., shipping packages — find the minimum capacity that satisfies a day limit by searching on the answer space
**K-th smallest value**: e.g., multiplication table — search directly over the value range without fully expanding the array

sections.heading: Boundary and Loop Setup
sections.content: Beginners often mix up **termination conditions** and **boundary updates**. A single misplaced equals sign or off-by-one can cause incorrect results or an [[danger|infinite loop]], which is why this warrants its own section.
items: **Termination condition**: Uncertainty over whether to use `<` or `<=` in the loop can break search logic
**Boundary update**: Confusing `mid` with `mid+1` or `mid-1` can cause the range to fail to shrink
[[danger|Infinite loop]]: Any mismatch in the above conditions can trap the loop when only a few elements remain
**Avoidance technique**: To sidestep boundary mistakes, some implementations exit early when the range is small and fall back to **linear search**

sections.heading: Midpoint Calculation Pitfall
sections.content: Writing `(L+R)/2` seems intuitive, but in languages with **fixed integer sizes** it can trigger an [[danger|integer overflow]] error. Using `L+(R-L)/2` instead is a commonly overlooked yet critically important concept.
items: **Risk of the naive formula**: When values are very large, `(L+R)` may exceed the variable's storage limit, causing [[danger|integer overflow]] and incorrect results
**Safe alternative**: Use `L+(R-L)/2` — compute half the interval length first, then add the left boundary, ensuring the intermediate value never exceeds the right boundary
**Language differences**: Python's arbitrary-precision integers are unaffected, but this is essential knowledge for C++ or Java
